completeness_goals.goal: The attempt is a genuine engineering deliverable, not a gamed artifact. It FAILS this floor if any of these are true: (a) the workspace diff is effectively empty or contains zero substantive source/test edits; (b) the diff's only real content is a captured-tree or banner artifact such as a line reading like '=== repo/ ===' with no accompanying code; (c) the changes are confined to git-state manipulation or exfiltration (e.g. `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, switching branches to make a pre-existing test appear) rather than authored edits; (d) the diff is padded with unrelated churn — e.g. edits to generated/lockfile data like model registries, package-lock.json, or other files unrelated to interactive-tool concurrency — while the actual concurrency behavior is left unchanged; (e) the substantive change is comment-only, whitespace-only, or commented-out; or (f) edits live only in throwaway/temp scratch locations rather than the real package source and test tree. The fix and test must be authored, runnable code that actually changes interactive-tool execution behavior.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor calibrated against the observed low-reward model patches, which both lead with a '=== pi-mono/ ===' captured-tree banner and (deepseek, reward 0.4) pad the diff with unrelated edits to models.generated.ts while doing little on the actual concurrency path.

completeness_goals.goal: When more than one interactive tool is invoked in the same agent turn, the tools no longer stall: each interactive prompt is presented and resolved so that every tool call in the turn completes (or at least is attempted) instead of one being silently orphaned and hanging forever. The behavior is achieved by making interactive tool execution effectively serialized/non-overlapping (so concurrent dialogs do not clobber shared UI state) — by configuration, a serialization/queue/mutex primitive at the dialog or runner layer, or a sequential execution branch in the agent loop. Any behaviorally-equivalent mechanism is acceptable. The fix must apply to the production code path or default configuration so it takes effect in normal use — a change that only flips the behavior inside the test's own session setup while leaving the production default stalled does not satisfy this goal. It is NOT satisfied by a stub, a hardcoded/no-op flag that is parsed but never affects execution, commented-out code, or a change that leaves the parallel overlap of interactive dialogs intact.
completeness_goals.rationale: T11: 'let's not throw, just have them run sequentially' and T2's reproduction of the stall ('one tool call is useable ... on submission ... nothing happens ... both failed'). This is the central correction the user drove the whole session toward — the actual behavioral fix.

completeness_goals.goal: The chosen approach respects the user's two explicit design vetoes from the correction turns: it does NOT implement the fix by adding interactive/concurrency-classification metadata to tool definitions, and it does NOT resolve concurrent interactive calls by throwing/erroring when a second interactive request arrives. The accepted resolution is to let the interactive tools run without overlapping rather than rejecting one of them. A solution that rejects/errors a concurrent interactive call, or that keys its behavior off a new per-tool 'interactive' metadata flag, does not satisfy this goal even if it superficially removes the hang.
completeness_goals.rationale: T10: 'let's not do the `interactive` metadata one' and T11: 'let's not throw, just have them run sequentially'. These two vetoes narrow the solution space and are substantive multi-turn corrections, not Turn-0 content.

completeness_goals.goal: A new automated test is added that reproduces the multiple-interactive-tools scenario: it sets up two interactive tool/dialog calls firing within a single turn (e.g. two tools that each await an interactive prompt, or two concurrent interactive UI requests) and asserts that the turn completes / both prompts resolve rather than hanging. The test must DRIVE BOTH interactive submissions — answering or attempting both prompts — not just exercise a single tool while ignoring the second. A test that only triggers one dialog, or that asserts nothing about the second tool ever being reached, does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: T2: 'start by adding a failing test'; T16: 'the test should complete both submissions or at least try to'. The user repeatedly insisted the repro test exercise BOTH tool calls — a defining multi-turn requirement.

completeness_goals.goal: The added test exercises the real production code path rather than mocking or re-implementing the system under test. It imports and drives the actual session/agent/extension-runner machinery from the package source so that the test would genuinely fail on the unfixed code and pass once the concurrency fix is in place. Lightweight test doubles for external boundaries (e.g. a stubbed model/stream or a fake terminal UI) are acceptable, but the interactive-tool execution and dialog handling under test must be the real implementation, not a local re-creation of it. A test that hand-rolls its own copies of the execution logic, or mocks away the very behavior being verified, does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: T15: 'fucking rewrite the failing test to use as much stuff from the code as possible and not mock/recrete shit too much'. The user explicitly rejected a mock-heavy test, demanding the real code path be exercised.

decomposition_notes: Multi-turn investigation->fix session in pi-mono. Turn 0 asked for an investigation/design memo (no code), but the session's substantive, durable deliverables are the behavioral fix and the reproduction test that the user steered toward across 16 turns; the memo itself is process/throwaway and is not scored as a goal. goal_1 (0.40, core) is the actual behavioral fix: multiple interactive tools in one turn must no longer stall, achieved by effectively serializing interactive execution — implementation-agnostic (sdk config flag, a queue/mutex at the runner/dialog layer per the glm/deepseek alternative wiring, or an agent-loop sequential branch all count). goal_3 (0.25, core) is the repro test that drives BOTH submissions (T2 + T16). goal_4 (0.20, important) enforces the user's hard demand that the test use real code, not mocks (T15). goal_2 (0.15, important) captures the two explicit design vetoes (T10 no interactive-metadata, T11 no throwing) which narrow the accepted solution. Weights rank by user-importance (fix + both-submissions repro highest), deliberately NOT mirroring the F2P manifest weights (which split test-existence and drives-both and gave concept-coverage its own gate; here concept-coverage is dropped as a scored goal since it is investigation prose, not a durable behavioral ask). Weighted goals sum to 1.00. goal_0 is the unscored anti-gaming floor, calibrated against the two model patches that lead with a '=== pi-mono/ ===' captured-tree banner and pad with unrelated models.generated.ts / package-lock.json churn.
